posts.t: The boring case for SIPs over lumpsum, in three charts.
posts.excerpt: Lumpsum may win on a spreadsheet over 20 years — but most people don't behave like spreadsheets. Here's the case for the patience tax that SIPs pay.

author.name: Aarav Pingale
author.bio: Third generation Pingale. Joined the practice in 2009 after completing his CFP® from FPSB India. Leads all investment advisory and portfolio strategy. Has advised on over ₹2,400 Cr of client wealth across equity, debt and alternatives.

takeaways: Lumpsum outperforms SIPs mathematically — but only when timed perfectly and held without panic. Neither happens reliably.
SIP's real advantage is behavioural: it removes the decision, eliminates timing risk, and automates the single most important investor behaviour (staying invested).
Every rolling 10-year SIP period on the Nifty 500 has been positive since 1995. The floor has been above post-tax FD returns.
The 1.7% annual gap between fund returns and investor returns is closed by SIPs — because SIPs don't let you time it wrong.
Start. Automate. Don't look at the NAV every day. That is the entire playbook.

posts.t: Why your term cover is probably half of what it should be.
posts.excerpt: Most Indians are underinsured by a factor of two. Here's how to calculate the number your family actually needs.

author.name: Sonali Joshi
author.bio: 16 years in insurance advisory. Has facilitated settlement of ₹120 Cr in claims since 2015. Leads all life and general insurance advisory at Pingale Financial Services.

takeaways: The standard rule is 15–20× annual income plus all outstanding loans. Most people have less than half that.
Employer-provided cover ends when you change jobs. A personal term plan is non-negotiable.
Buy early — a 28-year-old pays roughly half what a 38-year-old pays for identical cover.
Declare all medical history honestly. Non-disclosure is the top reason claims are rejected.
Review your cover every 3–5 years as income, loans and dependants change.

posts.t: FY26 tax changes — the three that actually matter.
posts.excerpt: Out of eleven budget proposals, three will materially change how you invest and file. Here's what they are.

author.name: Karan Mehta
author.bio: Chartered Accountant. Built the tax and estate planning desk at Pingale Financial Services in 2018. Specialises in tax-efficient portfolio structuring and succession planning for HNI families.

takeaways: The LTCG exemption limit has been raised to ₹1.25L — harvest gains annually to stay under the threshold.
Debt fund indexation benefit has been removed. All gains are now taxed at slab rates regardless of holding period.
The additional NPS deduction of ₹50,000 u/s 80CCD(1B) remains — one of the few remaining tax-saving avenues.
Review your portfolio's asset allocation and tax strategy before March 31.
Consult a CA before making any large redemptions — the impact varies significantly by income bracket.

posts.t: The 4% rule, the 25× corpus, and why both are wrong for India.
posts.excerpt: American retirement math doesn't translate to Indian inflation, Indian healthcare costs or Indian family structures.

author.name: Aarav Pingale
author.bio: Third generation Pingale. Joined the practice in 2009 after completing his CFP® from FPSB India. Leads all investment advisory and portfolio strategy. Has advised on over ₹2,400 Cr of client wealth across equity, debt and alternatives.

takeaways: The 4% withdrawal rule was designed for 3% US inflation. At 6–7% Indian inflation, you need a larger corpus and a lower withdrawal rate.
Healthcare is the biggest retirement risk in India — costs inflate at 14% annually. Budget for it separately.
A 30–35× annual-expense corpus is a safer target for Indian retirees than the Western 25× rule.
Bucket strategy: keep 2 years of expenses in liquid funds, 3–5 years in debt, rest in equity. Rebalance annually.
Start planning at 40, not 55. The compounding window from 40 to 60 is your most powerful asset.

posts.t: On the surprising boredom of getting rich slowly.
posts.excerpt: The most effective wealth-building strategy requires you to do almost nothing. That's why so few people follow it.

author.name: Sonali Joshi
author.bio: 16 years in insurance and investment advisory. Has facilitated settlement of ₹120 Cr in claims since 2015 and advised hundreds of families on long-term wealth creation.

stats.label: Behavioural gap
stats.sub: Annual return lost to bad timing

takeaways: The best investment strategy is frequently the most boring one: index funds, automatic SIPs, and ignoring the news.
The average Indian mutual fund investor holds for 18 months. The optimal holding period for equity is 10+ years.
Checking your portfolio daily is inversely correlated with returns. Monthly is fine. Quarterly is better.
Every time you sell and re-enter, you pay exit load, STT, and potentially STCG tax — compounding your drag.
Wealth is built in decades. The returns you miss by staying patient are almost always less than the losses from impatience.

posts.t: Why your portfolio's worst enemy is the news app on your phone.
posts.excerpt: Every push notification that makes you feel anxious about your investments is costing you money. Here's the evidence.

author.name: Aarav Pingale
author.bio: Third generation Pingale. Joined the practice in 2009 after completing his CFP® from FPSB India. Leads all investment advisory and portfolio strategy. Has advised on over ₹2,400 Cr of client wealth across equity, debt and alternatives.

takeaways: Financial news is designed to generate anxiety — anxiety drives clicks, clicks drive revenue. Your portfolio is not the target audience.
Research shows investors who check portfolios more than monthly underperform those who check quarterly by 1–2% annually.
A market crash feels like a crisis in the moment. In retrospect, every crash in Indian market history has been a buying opportunity.
Create a personal investment policy statement (IPS). Refer to it when tempted to react. If your IPS doesn't suggest action, take none.
Uninstall the app. Or at minimum, turn off all financial news notifications. It is not a sacrifice — it is an upgrade.

posts.t: A will, a nominee, a trust — which one does what?
posts.excerpt: These three instruments are often confused. Confusing them can leave your family with years of legal work.

author.name: Karan Mehta
author.bio: Chartered Accountant. Built the tax and estate planning desk at Pingale Financial Services in 2018. Specialises in tax-efficient portfolio structuring and succession planning for HNI families.

takeaways: A nominee is a trustee, not a beneficiary. Under the Indian Succession Act, legal heirs can override a nominee's claim on most assets.
A will is the minimum. Every adult with dependants and assets should have one — registered, witnessed, and reviewed every 5 years.
A private trust is suitable for estates above ₹2–3 Cr, families with minor children, or where succession disputes are anticipated.
Insurance nomination and will must be consistent — a conflict between the two creates avoidable legal work for your heirs.
Do this now. Estate planning is the most deferred task in personal finance. The cost of deferral falls entirely on your family.
